title: Chapter 3. Marginalia at 2:13 A.M.
content:
At 2:13 in the morning, the city buses stopped climbing the hill. The archive entered its truest kind of silence then, the kind in which every small motion acquired significance.

Elian turned the second black book over in her hands and found notes written in the margins of pages that were otherwise blank. The handwriting shifted from page to page. Some careful, some hurried, some full of extravagant loops that looked almost embarrassed by themselves.

Do not shelve this with the atlases.

The room remembers more than the readers do.

Check the west window when the rain moves sideways.

She copied each sentence into her notebook before daring to look up. The lamp seemed lower than before, or perhaps the ceiling higher. That was the difficulty of the archive after midnight. It did not always change objects. Sometimes it changed the confidence with which you measured them.

When rain struck the west window, it did not sound like weather. It sounded like fingertips, insistent and orderly.

Elian carried the notebook to the pane and found, on the fogged glass, a line traced from the outside in a single clean stroke.

RETURN WHAT WAS BORROWED.

She stared long enough for the letters to blur. Then, with a steadier hand than she felt, she wrote beneath them from the inside.

Tell me by whom.

The answer did not appear. But somewhere in the sealed wing, a shelf shifted with the long, wooden sigh of something making room.
